did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning framework named SkinNet-14, which is a modified compact convolutional transformer (CCT) architecture, to classify skin cancer types using low-resolution dermoscopy images. The model was trained and evaluated on dermatology datasets (HAM10000, ISIC, PAD) to achieve classification results.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' in the header. It presents original experimental work, introducing a novel deep learning model (SkinNet-14), describing its methodology (modified CCT architecture, preprocessing, augmentation), and reporting new results from training and evaluation on specific datasets (HAM10000, ISIC, PAD) with quantitative metrics (accuracy, training time). It is not a review or survey of prior literature.

title: SkinNet-14: a deep learning framework for accurate skin cancer classification using low-resolution dermoscopy images with optimized training time
brief_summary: This study introduces SkinNet-14, a novel deep learning framework based on a modified compact convolutional transformer (CCT) architecture, designed to classify skin cancer types using low-resolution (32x32 pixel) dermoscopy images. The model addresses computational efficiency and data imbalance by employing specific image preprocessing, augmentation strategies, and optimized layer designs. It was evaluated on three public datasets: HAM10000, ISIC, and PAD-UFES-20. The framework achieved high classification accuracies of 97.85%, 96.00%, and 98.14% on these respective datasets. Additionally, SkinNet-14 significantly reduced training time to 2–8 seconds per epoch while maintaining stability even with smaller training sets compared to traditional transfer learning models.

lesion_types_studied.details: Studied in HAM10000 (514 images), ISIC (376 images), and PAD-UFES-20 (845 images) datasets.
Studied in HAM10000 (1,113 images), ISIC (438 images), and PAD-UFES-20 (52 images) datasets. Described as the most aggressive form of skin cancer.

all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'skin cancer classification' using a deep learning framework (SkinNet-14) to predict discrete lesion classes (e.g., Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Nevus) from dermoscopy images. The model performs multiclass classification on datasets HAM10000, ISIC, and PAD-UFES-20, reporting accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A publicly available Kaggle dataset (Human against machine with 10,000 training images) containing 10,015 skin lesion images with a resolution of 644 x 450 pixels. It was chosen for initial model development due to its comprehensive image volume.

use.training: Used for initial model development; split into training, validation, and testing sets adhering to a 75:10:15 distribution.
use.validation: Used for validation as part of the 75:10:15 split.
use.testing: Used for testing as part of the 75:10:15 split.

datasets_used.name: ISIC
datasets_used.description: The ISIC-2019 (International Skin Imaging Collaboration) dataset, collected from the Kaggle database, containing 2,357 clinical and dermoscopic images with a resolution of 600 x 450 pixels. It was used to validate the model after initial development on HAM10000.

use.validation: Used for validation of the optimized model.
use.testing: Used for testing of the optimized model.

datasets_used.name: PAD-UFES-20
datasets_used.description: The PAD-UFES-20 (Dermatology image database from Federal University of Espirito Santo) dataset, comprising 2,298 images with a resolution of 1050 x 1050 pixels. It is commonly employed in model development due to its high quality, accuracy, and varying levels of difficulty.

use.validation: Used for validation of the optimized model.
use.testing: Used for testing of the optimized model.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To optimize model architecture and hyperparameters (e.g., transformer layers, activation functions, pooling layers, stride size, kernel size, loss function, optimizer, learning rate, image size) by comparing performance metrics across different configurations.
To evaluate classification performance, specifically identifying misclassifications (e.g., for Vascular Lesions) and calculating per-class metrics such as Precision, Recall, and F1-score.

statistical_methods_used.method: Performance metrics calculation (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-score)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To quantify the classification performance of the SkinNet-14 model on the HAM10000, ISIC, and PAD-UFES-20 datasets.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported in the paper.

HAM10000.notes: SkinNet-14 achieved 97.85% accuracy with a training time of 7-8 seconds per epoch. The model showed poorer performance for Vascular Lesions, with 100 misclassifications out of 107 images in the confusion matrix. The dataset was split into training, validation, and testing sets with a 75:10:15 distribution.

ISIC.notes: SkinNet-14 achieved 96.00% accuracy with a training time of 2-3 seconds per epoch. In the confusion matrix, 30 out of 58 Seborrheic Keratosis images were misclassified. The dataset was used for validation and testing of the optimized model.

PAD-UFES-20.notes: SkinNet-14 achieved 98.14% accuracy with a training time of 2-3 seconds per epoch. The dataset was used for validation and testing of the optimized model.

architecture_used.pretraining: Null (trained from scratch on dermatology datasets; base CCT parameters were used as initialization for ablation studies but final model optimized via ablation)

architecture_details.tokenization: Convolutional Tokenization (Conv2D with 64 filters, stride 2, ReLU, followed by MaxPool)
architecture_details.transformer_blocks: 1 Transformer Encoder Block (modified from base CCT which has 2)
architecture_details.sequence_pooling: Used to gather output of transformer backbone
architecture_details.regularization: Regularization layers applied after transformer blocks and sequence pooling

training_details.hardware: Intel Core i5-8400, 16 GB RAM, NVidia GeForce GTX 1660 GPU

classification_or_output_head.output_dimension: Number of classes (varies by dataset: 7 for HAM10000, 9 for ISIC, 6 for PAD-UFES-20)

preprocessing_techniques: Morphological opening (using a 10x10 kernel) to remove single-pixel artifacts and noisy spikes
Non-Local Means Denoising (NLMD) to minimize noise while preserving crucial image information
Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) to increase brightness and contrast
Gaussian Blur (using cv2.bilateralFilter with diameter 9, sigmaColor 75, sigmaSpace 75) to smooth pixels and minimize noise
Downsizing to 32x32 pixels for model input
Segmentation approaches (thresholding, edge detection, region-based) were experimented with but not used as they did not increase performance

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Included vertical flipping, horizontal flipping, and rotation (clockwise and anticlockwise 90 degrees). No additional parameters required. Applied to increase dataset volume.

Included changing brightness, contrast, color, and sharpness. A factor value of 1.5 was used to generate optimum augmentation without compromising pixel information. This technique yielded the best test accuracy (89.85%) in ablation studies.

Stretching and changing image shape based on skin location and compression strength. Parameters: maximum random displacement (m) selected from range [0.5, 0.5] consistently for each pixel; Gaussian filter standard deviation (r) set to 300 for smoothing; deformation degree set to 20. Used order one spline interpolation for intensity mapping.

Geometric, photometric, and elastic deformations were applied to address gross class imbalance in the datasets. Table 1 shows data counts increased from original to augmented (e.g., HAM10000 from 10,015 to 50,075 images per technique).

conclusions: SkinNet-14, a modified compact convolutional transformer (CCT) architecture, effectively classifies skin cancer types using low-resolution (32x32 pixel) dermoscopy images, addressing the need for reduced computational load and training duration compared to models requiring high-resolution inputs.
The proposed framework achieved high classification accuracies of 97.85% on HAM10000, 96.00% on ISIC, and 98.14% on PAD-UFES-20 datasets.

SkinNet-14 significantly reduces training time to 2–8 seconds per epoch while maintaining stability and performance even with smaller training sets, making it suitable for resource-constrained clinical settings.

The model outperforms traditional transfer learning models (including ResNet50, ResNet152, MobileNet, ResNet50V2, VGG16, and VGG19) in both accuracy and computational efficiency across all tested datasets.

The study demonstrates that transformer-based models can be efficient and effective for medical image analysis when optimized with specific preprocessing and augmentation strategies to handle data imbalance and noise.

The model shows limitations in classifying specific classes with high inter-class similarity or low sample sizes, such as Vascular Lesions in the HAM10000 dataset and Seborrheic Keratosis in the ISIC dataset, as evidenced by confusion matrix analysis.
important_remarks: The model was trained and evaluated exclusively on three public datasets (HAM10000, ISIC, PAD-UFES-20); there is no external validation on independent clinical data or real-world deployment evidence.
The study addresses class imbalance through data augmentation rather than native loss weighting or sampling strategies, which may not reflect true prevalence in clinical populations.

Performance was significantly lower for specific classes with high inter-class similarity or low sample sizes, such as Vascular Lesions in HAM10000 (100 misclassifications out of 107) and Seborrheic Keratosis in ISIC (30 out of 58 misclassified).

The model processes low-resolution (32x32 pixel) images, which may limit the extraction of fine-grained diagnostic features compared to high-resolution inputs, potentially affecting sensitivity for subtle lesion characteristics.
The paper does not report statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for the performance differences between SkinNet-14 and the baseline models.

The dataset splits are not fully detailed for ISIC and PAD-UFES-20 (training/validation/testing counts are null), raising potential concerns about data leakage or non-standard evaluation protocols for these specific datasets.
The hardware used for training (Intel Core i5-8400, 16 GB RAM, GTX 1660) is consumer-grade, which may not represent performance in high-throughput clinical environments.
The authors acknowledge that future work should focus on extracting features from a larger set of raw photos and real-time data collection, indicating current limitations in generalizability.
